Task id: T88 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: 8c0411d (main) — FIRST run `git reset --hard 8c0411d` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T88`.

Go repo (wanbond). Gate: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`, plus `go test -race ./internal/bind/...`.

CONTEXT: internal/bind now has (T83-T87): peerState with per-peer psk/codec/reflector (T84), peerByVirt Send routing (T85), per-peer receive drain (T86), per-peer Open lifecycle + setPeerRemoteLocked (T87), sharedPathState/peerPathState split with peerBySource/peerByEndpoint placeholder maps (T83). handleInbound currently routes via the peerPathState's ps.peer back-reference; source->peer binding is what YOU add.

headline: Bind source->peer only via authenticated PROBE with trial-decode across peer PSKs

description: Build the authenticated path->peer binding: an inbound PROBE from an unbound source is trial-decoded against each configured peer's psk-derived codec/reflector (O(peers), bounded by the static peer count), and on the first successful MAC verification the source address is bound to that peer (source->peer map). Only an authenticated PROBE ever establishes a binding; unauthenticated frames verify under no psk and are dropped cheaply. No DATA/PARITY wire change; no peer id in DATA. (Preserve the existing single-peer fast path: with one bound peer the current handleInbound behavior must stay byte-identical — trial-decode only matters when >1 peer is registered. Preserve the D9/D11 discipline: remotes learned only from authenticated PROBEs.)

acceptance: Unit test: a PROBE encoded under peer B's psk from a fresh source binds that source to B (and reflects an echo); a forged/garbage frame verifies under no peer psk and establishes no binding; trial-decode stops at the first matching psk. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.

TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.

- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1074		// edge/hub ps.peer is the embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the singleton.
1075		pr := ps.peer
1076		switch f := fr.(type) {
1077		case frame.Data:
1078			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1079			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1080			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1081			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1082				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1083				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1084				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1085				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1086				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1087				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1088				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1089				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1090				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1091				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1092				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1093				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1094					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1095				}
1096				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1097			} else {
1098				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1099			}
1100		case frame.Parity:
1101			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1102			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1103			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1104			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1105			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1106			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1107				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1108				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1109				m.observeRecovered(fr, pr.resequencer.Load(), recovered, srcAP)
1110			}
1111		case frame.Probe:
1112			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1113			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1114			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1115			if f.IsEcho {
1116				if ps.prober != nil {
1117					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1118					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1119					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1120					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1121					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1122				}
1123				return
1124			}
1125			if pr.reflector != nil {
1126				if echo, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
1127					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
1128					// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
1129					_, _ = ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP)
1130				}
1131			}
1132		default:
1133			// CONTROL (and any unhandled kind) is not delivered up this path.
1134		}
1135	}
1136	
1137	// observeRecovered feeds every FEC-reconstructed data frame into the resequencer at
1138	// its ORIGINAL outer-seq (recovered from the shard's coded bytes) via the NON-resyncing
1139	// ObserveRecovered path, so a frame rebuilt from parity resequences identically to a
1140	// natively-received frame — filling the exact gap the resequencer would otherwise time
1141	// out on — WITHOUT a late batch of recovered seqs being able to move the release point
1142	// or dump the live buffer (recovery must never cause loss). Only frames actually placed
1143	// AHEAD of the release point advance the honest delivered-recovery counter; a frame
1144	// rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and not
1145	// counted, so /metrics reflects delivered recovery, not mere reconstruction. A
1146	// malformed recovered shard (too short to hold the outer-seq prefix) is dropped: it
1147	// signals an encoder/decoder mismatch, not a deliverable frame.
1148	func (m *Multipath) observeRecovered(fr *fecReceiver, rq *reseq.Resequencer, recovered []fec.Recovered, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1149		for _, rec := range recovered {
1150			seq, inner, err := splitFECShardPayload(rec.Payload)
1151			if err != nil {
1152				continue
1153			}
1154			// Residual-loss accounting (T29): FEC reconstructed this outer-seq, so it is NOT
1155			// residual loss — mark it present in the post-recovery estimator even if the
1156			// resequencer later drops it as late (that is a latency outcome, not a masking
1157			// failure; the P4 residual bound measures loss FEC failed to mask).
1158			if fr.connLoss != nil {
1159				fr.connLoss.Observe(seq)
1160			}
1161			if rq.ObserveRecovered(seq, inner, srcAP) {
1162				fr.deliveredRecovered.Add(1)
1163			}
1164		}
1165	}
1166	
1167	// virtualEndpoint returns the single stable endpoint the engine holds for the GIVEN
1168	// peer (ps). Each peer owns its OWN virtual endpoint (invariant A1: one virtual endpoint
1169	// per peer), so the drainer stamps a delivered inner datagram with the endpoint of the
1170	// peer whose resequencer released it — the engine thus attributes return traffic to the
1171	// right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's virt. On a peer with no
1172	// configured endpoint (the concentrator) its destination is pinned ONCE to the first
1173	// learned source; thereafter every path returns the identical pointer so the engine sees
1174	// one peer, never per-packet churn.
1175	//
1176	// Hot-path note: the destination, once pinned, never changes, and it is published
1177	// through an atomic.Pointer (see udpEndpoint). So the common case takes a
1178	// lock-free fast path — every received datagram would otherwise contend m.mu with
1179	// in-flight Sends. The mutex is acquired only to pin the FIRST learned source.
1180	func (m *Multipath) virtualEndpoint(ps *peerState, learned netip.AddrPort) Endpoint {
1181		if ps.virt.dstValid() {
1182			return ps.virt
1183		}
1184		m.mu.Lock()
1185		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1186		if !ps.virt.dstValid() {
1187			ps.virt.setDst(learned)
1188		}
1189		return ps.virt
1190	}
1191	
1192	// Send wraps each buffer in an outer DATA frame (fresh outer-seq + the chosen
1193	// path's id) and writes it to that path's remote. The egress path is chosen by
1194	// the injected scheduler (T15): the active-backup policy returns the preferred
1195	// primary while it is up and the failover backup otherwise. The scheduler
1196	// selects by path priority/liveness only; the Bind additionally requires THAT
1197	// ONE chosen path to have a known remote, failing the send otherwise rather
1198	// than silently dropping.
1199	//
1200	// Behavioural change from pre-T15: this is a deliberate NARROWING. The removed
1201	// pickPathLocked iterated the paths and skipped any healthy-but-remoteless one,
1202	// falling through to the next healthy path WITH a known remote, so a send failed
1203	// only when NO path had a remote. Now the scheduler owns selection and returns a
1204	// single index; the Bind does NOT fall through, because a Bind-level fall-through
1205	// would bypass the scheduler's hysteresis (failover/failback). The residual
1206	// window this opens: if the scheduler's chosen path is reported Up but its remote
1207	// is not yet learned — e.g. a concentrator/hub restart, or a T16 NAT-rebind
1208	// before the first inbound packet re-teaches the remote — the send fails until
1209	// that path's remote is learned, even if another path has a known remote.
1210	//
1211	// Critical-section discipline: path selection and framing run under m.mu (the
1212	// send Codec is shared and stateful — D5's single keystream requires sequential,
1213	// mutex-guarded Encode), but each datagram is encoded into its OWN fresh buffer
1214	// so it outlives the lock, and the WriteToUDPAddrPort syscalls run WITHOUT m.mu
1215	// held. The scheduler is internally synchronized and never calls back into the
1216	// Bind, so calling Pick under m.mu cannot deadlock and adds no receive-path
1217	// contention (the lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path is untouched). A receive
1218	// goroutine pinning the virtual endpoint therefore never blocks behind an
1219	// in-flight transmit syscall.
1220	func (m *Multipath) Send(bufs [][]byte, ep Endpoint) error {
1221		ue, ok := ep.(*udpEndpoint)
1222		if !ok {
1223			return conn.ErrWrongEndpointType
1224		}
1225	
1226		// Classify the batch by inner WireGuard message type (defect D22): a handshake or
1227		// keepalive is passed to the scheduler as ClassControl so a pacing scheduler exempts
1228		// it from the per-path data token buckets and bulk overload cannot shed it and starve
1229		// rekey. The classifier is parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia profile, so it works
1230		// under advanced-security obfuscation (custom magic headers + handshake junk), not
1231		// only vanilla WireGuard. It reads only the (possibly junk-shifted) type word off the
1232		// lock. It is peer-agnostic (inner WireGuard type, not routing), so it runs before the
1233		// peer resolution below.
1234		class := m.classify.classifyBatch(bufs)
1235	
1236		m.mu.Lock()
1237		// Resolve the OWNING peer from the engine-facing virtual endpoint: each peer holds a
1238		// DISTINCT virt, so this datagram egresses on THAT peer's outerSeq, scheduler,
1239		// sendCodec, fecSend, and per-(peer,path) set — never the embedded primary's by
1240		// promotion. An endpoint of the right type but unknown to the demux (no bound peer)
1241		// cannot be routed, so it returns the no-path error rather than misrouting onto some
1242		// other peer's paths. On the single-peer edge/hub the primary's virt resolves to the
1243		// primary peerState, so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton.
1244		peer, ok := m.peerByVirt[ue]
1245		if !ok {
1246			m.mu.Unlock()
1247			return errNoHealthyPath
1248		}
1249		if len(peer.paths) == 0 {
1250			m.mu.Unlock()
1251			return errClosed
1252		}
1253		idx := peer.scheduler.Pick(class)
1254		if idx == sched.PickPaced {
1255			// The scheduler shed this datagram for pacing while paths are healthy: drop it
1256			// (same as no-path), but surface a DISTINCT error so the diagnostic is not
1257			// conflated with a total outage. The rate-limited log is emitted at the source
1258			// (the scheduler), so no per-drop logging happens here on the hot path.
1259			m.mu.Unlock()
1260			return errPacerShedding
1261		}
1262		if idx < 0 || idx >= len(peer.paths) {
1263			m.mu.Unlock()
1264			return errNoHealthyPath
1265		}
1266		ps := peer.paths[idx]
1267		remote, ok := ps.getRemote()
1268		if !ok {
1269			m.mu.Unlock()
1270			return errNoHealthyPath
1271		}
1272		c := ps.conn
1273		wires := make([]fecWire, 0, len(bufs))
1274		for _, b := range bufs {
1275			seq := peer.outerSeq.Add(1)
1276			if peer.fecSend != nil {
1277				// FEC on (T24): admit the inner datagram (coded as seq || inner) to the group
1278				// encoder. The returned data shard rides a normal DATA frame carrying its FEC
1279				// group + shard index; when this admission FILLS the group the encoder returns
1280				// the group's parity shards, emitted here as KindParity frames on the SAME
1281				// chosen path. Spreading parity onto a DIFFERENT path than its data (so one
1282				// path outage cannot lose both) is a documented future refinement, deliberately
1283				// NOT implemented here (see the T24 design notes).
1284				ds, parity, err := peer.fecSend.enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, b))
1285				if err != nil {
1286					m.mu.Unlock()
1287					return err
1288				}
1289				wire, err := peer.sendCodec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: ps.id, FECGroup: uint32(ds.Group), FECIndex: uint8(ds.Index), Payload: b})
1290				if err != nil {
1291					m.mu.Unlock()
1292					return err
1293				}
1294				wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: wire})
1295				for _, par := range parity {
1296					pw, err := m.encodeParityLocked(peer, par, ps.id)
1297					if err != nil {
1298						m.mu.Unlock()
1299						return err
1300					}
1301					wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: pw, parity: true})
1302				}
1303				continue
1304			}
1305			wire, err := peer.sendCodec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: ps.id, Payload: b})
1306			if err != nil {
1307				m.mu.Unlock()
1308				return err
1309			}
1310			wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: wire})
1311		}
1312		fs := peer.fecSend
1313		m.mu.Unlock()
1314	
1315		for _, w := range wires {
1316			if _, err := c.WriteToUDPAddrPort(w.b, remote); err != nil {
1317				return err
1318			}
1319			// Per-path egress-wire accounting (T23): count the OUTER frame bytes just written
1320			// to this path (DATA and any FEC PARITY alike), lock-free and only for a datagram
1321			// that actually reached the socket. Send serialized the path choice under m.mu, so
1322			// ps is fixed here and this is the sole writer of ps.txBytes for this frame.
1323			ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1324			if fs != nil {
1325				// FEC frame accounting (T24/T25), counted only once the frame reached the socket
1326				// so the /metrics overhead ratio reflects wire cost actually spent. Parity and
1327				// DATA are charged to disjoint counters; their ratio ParityFrames/DataFrames is
1328				// the fixed-ratio overhead the P3 e2e asserts against M/K. fs != nil only when FEC
1329				// is enabled, so a plain (FEC-off) datapath increments neither counter.
1330				if w.parity {
1331					fs.parityFrames.Add(1)
1332					fs.parityBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1333				} else {
1334					fs.dataFrames.Add(1)
1335					// DATA-frame wire bytes: the overhead-BYTES denominator (T29). The P4
1336					// acceptance compares parity BYTES / data BYTES, so both are counted on the
1337					// same only-once-it-reached-the-socket basis as the frame counters.
1338					fs.dataBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1339				}
1340			}
1341		}
1342		return nil
1343	}

132	// peerState holds the per-PEER datapath state that was a process-global singleton before
133	// the multi-peer concentrator split: the SINGLE virtual endpoint the engine holds for this
134	// peer, the peer's send scheduler, its own outer-seq space, its shared send Codec, its
135	// probe reflector, its receive resequencer and FEC send/receive planes, its per-path probe
136	// initiators, and its per-(peer,path) views over the shared sockets. The single-peer
137	// edge/hub constructs EXACTLY ONE peerState (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split
138	// singleton — Multipath embeds it as the primary and the datapath reaches its fields through
139	// that embed); the concentrator constructs one peerState per bound peer. resequencer and
140	// fecRecv stay atomic.Pointer, and outerSeq atomic, so the lock-free receive/send fast paths
141	// read them WITHOUT m.mu.
142	type peerState struct {
143		// name is the peer id/name, the key under which Multipath.peersByName holds this peer.
144		// Empty on the single-peer edge/hub (there is only one peer to key).
145		name string
146	
147		// psk is THIS peer's effective pre-shared key: the sole seam from which every frame
148		// Codec this peer derives (its sendCodec and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via
149		// newCodec) and its probe Reflector authenticate. The single-peer edge/hub sets it to
150		// the one configured psk (so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton);
151		// the concentrator sets a DIFFERENT psk per bound peer, so one peer's codec/reflector
152		// rejects another peer's frames (T84). NewCodec/NewReflector PSK-derivation itself is
153		// unchanged — only WHICH psk each peer feeds them differs.
154		psk config.Key
155	
156		// Immutable per-peer collaborators, built at construction and persisting across the
157		// Open→Close socket lifecycle (the concentrator pins virt's destination once for the
158		// process life, so virt in particular must NOT be recreated per Open).
159		virt      *udpEndpoint
160		scheduler sched.Scheduler
161		reflector *telemetry.Reflector
162		// newProber mints a prober for a path admitted to THIS peer at runtime (T30); nil
163		// disables runtime path addition for the peer (a bind without the probe transport).
164		newProber ProberFactory
165		// probers is this peer's BOOT-TIME per-path probe initiator set, in durable-membership
166		// (m.defs) order — bound AND deferred — shared with this peer's scheduler. At Open each
167		// bound entry is bound onto its peerPathState (pp.prober), and thereafter the hot paths
168		// reach a path's prober through the peerPathState — never by indexing this slice — so a
169		// runtime path add/remove cannot race echo handling. Nil when the peer runs without the
170		// probe transport; a nil here also gates the whole probe loop off.
171		probers []*telemetry.Prober
172	
173		// Per-Open state, (re)built by Open and cleared by Close.
174		sendCodec *frame.Codec
175		paths     []*peerPathState
176		outerSeq  atomic.Uint64
177		// resequencer is this peer's shared T18 receive resequencing buffer. Published
178		// atomically so the per-path readLoop goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.
179		resequencer atomic.Pointer[reseq.Resequencer]
180		// FEC datapath (T24), per peer. fecSend accessed only under m.mu; fecRecv published
181		// atomically like resequencer. Both nil/empty when FEC is off.
182		fecSend *fecSender
183		fecRecv atomic.Pointer[fecReceiver]
184	}
185	
186	// newPeerState builds the durable per-peer datapath state whose probe Reflector — and,
187	// through ps.psk, every frame Codec this peer later derives in Open/AddPath (its sendCodec
188	// and each per-(peer,path) receive codec, via newCodec) — authenticate under THIS peer's
189	// psk. It is the seam that replaces the pre-split single-psk assumption (T84): the
190	// single-peer edge/hub mints exactly one (the primary) from the sole configured psk, while
191	// the concentrator mints one per bound peer from that peer's DIFFERENT effective psk, so a
192	// peerState built from a different psk gets its own codec/reflector and cross-psk frames are
193	// rejected. virt is created here (not per Open) because the concentrator pins its destination
194	// once for the process life. The Reflector draws its per-path challenges from crypto/rand,
195	// exactly as before. The per-Open fields (sendCodec, paths, resequencer, FEC planes) are
196	// left zero for Open to (re)build.
197	func newPeerState(name string, psk config.Key, scheduler sched.Scheduler, newProber ProberFactory, probers []*telemetry.Prober) *peerState {
198		return &peerState{
199			name:      name,
200			psk:       psk,
201			virt:      &udpEndpoint{},
202			scheduler: scheduler,
203			reflector: telemetry.NewReflector(psk, rand.Reader),
204			newProber: newProber,
205			probers:   probers,
206		}
207	}
208	
209	// newCodec derives a fresh frame Codec bound to THIS peer's psk — the send Codec at Open and
210	// each per-(peer,path) receive Codec share the derivation but not the instance, since a Codec
211	// is not safe for concurrent use (each receive path decodes on its own goroutine). Deriving
212	// from ps.psk (not a Multipath-wide key) is what makes a path's receive Codec the codec of the
213	// peer the path is bound to (T84).
214	func (ps *peerState) newCodec() (*frame.Codec, error) {

218	// deferredPath is a configured path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr was not yet

243	}

328		peerBySource   map[netip.Addr]*peerState

358		hasDefaultRemote bool

365		adaptiveCfg *adaptivefec.Config

380		// state.

843			ps.rxBytes.Add(uint64(n))

853			select {

862	// the starvation-robust companion to StartProbeLoop's timer-driven Tick: the timer
863	// goroutine can be delayed ~1s under CPU saturation, but the receive goroutines are
864	// scheduled by the very traffic that must trigger failover, so liveness advances
865	// regardless. Ticking is monotone-safe: Tick only marks an UP path DOWN once its
866	// silence STRICTLY exceeds DownAfter and never brings a path UP (that needs
867	// RecordEcho), so a more frequent Tick can only make a genuine DOWN transition land
868	// sooner — never a premature/false one — and the failback hysteresis (owned by the
869	// scheduler) is untouched. No-op when the probe transport is absent (interval unset).
870	func (m *Multipath) tickLivenessFromReceive(now time.Time) {
871		interval := m.sweepIntervalNanos.Load()
872		if interval == 0 {
873			return // no probe transport / loop not started: nothing to Tick
874		}
875		n := now.UnixNano()
876		last := m.lastSweepNanos.Load()

883		// Snapshot the prober set under m.mu (a runtime AddPath/RemovePath mutates it),
884		// then Tick OUTSIDE the lock so a transition's log write never runs under m.mu.
885		// TryLock, NOT Lock: Close holds m.mu WHILE it waits on readersWG for the readers to
886		// exit, so a reader that BLOCKED here on m.mu would deadlock that shutdown. The sweep
887		// is opportunistic and throttled, so when the lock is contended (a concurrent
888		// Close/AddPath/RemovePath/Send) simply skipping this interval is harmless — the
889		// probe-loop ticker and the next receive still advance liveness. This preserves
890		// Close's invariant that a reader never blocks on m.mu. The lock, when taken, is held
891		// at most once per interval (~5/s) for a bounded snapshot, so it adds negligible
892		// contention to Send and does not disturb the lock-free receive fast path.
893		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
894			return
895		}
896		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, 0, len(m.paths))
897		for _, ps := range m.paths {
898			if ps.prober != nil {
899				probers = append(probers, ps.prober)
900			}
901		}
902		m.mu.Unlock()
903		for _, pr := range probers {
904			pr.Tick()
905		}
906		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18, the repeated-flap wedge). The active-backup
907		// selection is otherwise recomputed ONLY inside the scheduler's Pick, which the
908		// Bind calls ONLY from Send — so failover is pull-based: it happens only when the
909		// engine hands down an egress datagram. When the ACTIVE path dies during an egress
910		// LULL that is fatal: a repeated-flap kill landing on the just-restored primary
911		// before the saturating flow re-fills it (the failback and the next kill overlap)
912		// leaves both TCP directions stalled on the now-dead path, so NO Send occurs, so
913		// Pick is never called, so egress is never switched to the healthy backup — the
914		// bond wedges on the dead path until the 25s WG keepalive finally drives a Send.
915		// The receive tick is the starvation-robust signal that ALREADY detected the DOWN
916		// (it just Ticked the path down above); recomputing the scheduler from it makes the
917		// switch EAGER — bounded by the detection window (~DownAfter + one interval) rather
918		// than by the next application Send. Pick recomputes purely against current liveness
919		// and the clock and the failback dwell is time-based, so a more frequent recompute
920		// can only make a genuine transition land sooner — never a premature/false failover,
921		// and never a shortened anti-thrash hysteresis. It takes ONLY the scheduler's own
922		// lock (never m.mu), so it adds no receive-path m.mu contention and cannot invert
923		// the Send-path m.mu→scheduler lock order.
924		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
925	}
926	
927	// nudgeSchedulerActive forces the scheduler to recompute its active egress path
928	// against current liveness, independent of any application Send. It is the eager-
929	// failover companion to the Send-driven Pick: driven from the liveness-detection
930	// paths (the receive tick and the probe loop) it switches egress to a healthy backup
931	// the moment the active path is detected DOWN, so a dead active path with stalled
932	// egress does not wedge the bond until the next Send (defect D18). The returned index
933	// is intentionally discarded — only the recompute (and its logged transition) is
934	// wanted here; Send remains the sole reader of the selection for actual routing. Pick
935	// is internally synchronized and never calls back into the Bind, so this is safe to
936	// call from a receive goroutine or the probe loop without m.mu.
937	//
938	// It calls Recompute, NOT Pick: the nudge wants only the liveness-driven active-set
939	// recompute (and its logged transition), and a weighted/aggregating scheduler's Pick
940	// is STATEFUL — a spurious Pick here would consume a distribution slot and skew the
941	// per-path weight split. Recompute is the non-consuming half that refreshes the
942	// eligible/active set without touching distribution/pacing/load state, so the T40
943	// eager-failover guarantee holds for BOTH the active-backup and the weighted policy
944	// (defect D18).

952	// attributes return traffic to the right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's

957	// reader feeds one of these resequencers and a single drainer releases them, a path (or

1632	// Close tears down every per-path socket and CLEARS the bind's path state so a
1633	// subsequent Open fully rebuilds it. The amneziawg engine drives exactly this
1634	// lifecycle — device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked calls Close() before
1635	// every Open(), and a Down/Up cycles Close after Open — so Close must leave the
1636	// bind reopenable (matching conn.StdNetBind, whose closed state is simply "no
1637	// sockets"). Outstanding receive calls return an error as their socket closes.
1638	func (m *Multipath) Close() error {
1639		m.mu.Lock()
1640		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1641		// Release the fan-in first so any reader parked on a delivery poke, and the
1642		// engine-facing drainer, unblock; then close the sockets so every readLoop
1643		// returns; then wait for them all to exit before clearing state so no reader
1644		// outlives this Close (and no reader touches the resequencer a later Open
1645		// replaces). readLoop needs no lock, so waiting under m.mu cannot deadlock.
1646		if m.recvClosed != nil {
1647			close(m.recvClosed)
1648		}
1649		err := m.closeSocketsLocked()
1650		m.readersWG.Wait()
1651		m.deliverSignal = nil
1652		m.recvClosed = nil
1653		return err
1654	}
1655	
1656	// closeSocketsLocked closes all path sockets and resets the bind to the unopened
1657	// state (paths and send Codec cleared), returning the first close error. Caller
1658	// holds m.mu. Idempotent: safe on an already-closed or never-opened bind — which
1659	// is exactly what the engine's pre-open Close relies on.
1660	func (m *Multipath) closeSocketsLocked() error {
1661		var firstErr error
1662		// Sockets are SHARED (one per shared path), so close them once from the shared list —
1663		// NOT once per (peer,path) view, which would double-close a concentrator socket.
1664		for _, sp := range m.shared {
1665			if sp.conn != nil {
1666				if err := sp.conn.Close(); err != nil && firstErr == nil {
1667					firstErr = err
1668				}
1669			}
1670		}
1671		m.shared = nil
1672		m.deferred = nil
1673		// Clear every peer's per-Open state so the next Open rebuilds it fresh (paths, send
1674		// Codec, FEC send/receive). The deadline-tick goroutine that reads a peer's fecSend is
1675		// stopped by Close (recvClosed) and joined via readersWG before state is cleared, and it
1676		// captured its own fecSender pointer, so niling here never races it.
1677		for _, p := range m.peers {
1678			p.paths = nil
1679			p.sendCodec = nil
1680			p.fecSend = nil
1681			p.fecRecv.Store(nil)
1682		}
1683		return firstErr
1684	}
1685	
1686	// AddPath admits a new path to the running bond at runtime (T30): it binds the
1687	// path's source-addr'd socket, mints its prober (via the injected factory) joining
1688	// the current probe session, seeds its remote from the config dest_addr or the
1689	// learned default, admits it to the scheduler as a NEW LOWEST-PRIORITY path, and
1690	// spawns its Bind-owned reader. Everything runs under m.mu, so the path slice and
1691	// the scheduler's path list are mutated together and stay index-aligned as Send
1692	// observes them (Send also holds m.mu). The new path starts DOWN (its prober has no
1693	// echoes yet) and is only selected once its probes report healthy, so admission
1694	// disturbs neither the active selection of the surviving paths nor the WG session:
1695	// the single virtual endpoint is untouched, and the engine's receive set does not
1696	// change (the reader is the Bind's, not the engine's).
1697	//
1698	// Path-ids are handed out monotonically from a high-water counter that persists
1699	// ACROSS Open spans (a Close->Open cycle does NOT reset it -- only a process restart
1700	// does), so a surviving path is never renumbered and a reopened bond never re-mints
1701	// an id the peer still associates with old per-path reflector state; the uint8 id
1702	// space (256 ids) is therefore consumed CUMULATIVELY over the process lifetime by
1703	// the initial-Open admissions (the N configured paths take ids 0..N-1) PLUS every
1704	// runtime AddPath admission combined, and is exhausted once 256 distinct ids have
1705	// been minted over the daemon's life, which fails fast rather than reusing an id and
1706	// colliding with the peer's per-path reflector state.
1707	func (m *Multipath) AddPath(def config.Path) error {
1708		m.mu.Lock()
1709		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1710		if len(m.paths) == 0 {
1711			return errClosed // only a running bind can take a runtime path
1712		}
1713		if m.newProber == nil {
1714			return errors.New("bind: cannot add a path at runtime without the probe transport")
1715		}
1716		if _, ok := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler); !ok {
1717			return errors.New("bind: scheduler does not support runtime path membership")
1718		}
1719		if !def.SourceAddr.IsValid() {
1720			return fmt.Errorf("bind: add path %q: source_addr is required", def.Name)
1721		}

125	// promoteDeferredLocked turns a just-bound deferred path into a LIVE one under m.mu. It
126	// wraps the fresh socket in a pathState that REUSES the deferred path's preserved boot
127	// prober — so the promoted path keeps its reserved path-id STAMP (no renumber, no
128	// collision with the peer's per-path reflector state), the continuity T51 preserved for
129	// exactly this step — seeds its remote from the config dest_addr or the learned default,
130	// appends it to m.paths, admits its prober to the scheduler as the new lowest-priority
131	// path, and spawns its Bind-owned reader. It mirrors AddPath's admission MINUS minting a
132	// prober and MINUS touching the durable membership (a deferred path is ALREADY in
133	// m.defs/m.probers and its id-stamp is ALREADY reserved in m.nextPathID by Open), so the
134	// caller need only drop it from m.deferred on success. It rolls a failed admission back
135	// completely and returns the error, leaving m.paths and the scheduler as it found them.
136	func (m *Multipath) promoteDeferredLocked(dyn sched.DynamicScheduler, dp deferredPath, c *net.UDPConn) error {
137		// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort (kernel-capped, needs no privilege) — as in Open/AddPath.
138		_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
139		// The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today, so this attaches the primary peer's
140		// view; its receive codec is that peer's codec, reached through the embedded primary.
141		codec, err := m.newCodec()
142		if err != nil {
143			return err // programmer error (psk validated at construction); never hit in practice
144		}
145		// Reuse the boot prober's IMMUTABLE stamp for the DATA-frame path-id, exactly as Open
146		// and AddPath do, so DATA and PROBE agree on the wire and the promoted path is never
147		// renumbered. The deferred-path machinery is single-peer today (the concentrator fan-out
148		// of a promoted deferred path is a later G4 task), so this attaches the primary peer's
149		// view over the freshly-bound shared socket.
150		shared := &sharedPathState{name: dp.def.Name, id: dp.prober.PathID(), src: dp.def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
151		ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec, prober: dp.prober}
152		switch {
153		case dp.def.DestAddr.IsValid():
154			ps.setRemote(dp.def.DestAddr)
155		case m.hasDefaultRemote:
156			ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
157		}
158		// Append to the shared socket list + the peer's path slice, then admit the prober to the
159		// scheduler as the new tail; the peer's path slice and its scheduler are index-aligned,
160		// so the scheduler's returned index must equal the new path's slice index. A mismatch
161		// would mis-route datagrams, so fail loudly and roll back.
162		m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
163		m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
164		schedIdx, err := dyn.AddPath(ps.prober)
165		if err != nil {
166			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
167			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
168			return err
169		}
170		if schedIdx != len(m.paths)-1 {
171			bindIdx := len(m.paths) - 1
172			_ = dyn.RemovePath(schedIdx)
173			m.paths = m.paths[:len(m.paths)-1]
174			m.shared = m.shared[:len(m.shared)-1]
175			return fmt.Errorf("bind: scheduler/path index skew after deferred promote: sched=%d bind=%d", schedIdx, bindIdx)
176		}
177		// Spawn the promoted path's Bind-owned reader on the CURRENT Open span's delivery
178		// signal; readersWG tracks it so a subsequent Close joins it (no goroutine leak).
179		m.readersWG.Add(1)
180		go m.readLoop(ps, m.deliverSignal)

182	}

header logic uses package-level globals). Config validation enforces the
   former; deployments must honor the latter.
7. **Re-verify the reedsolomon prefix invariant** on any FEC-dependency bump.

## Security model

- **Payload**: confidentiality, integrity, authenticity provided by inner
  WireGuard (Noise + AEAD). wanbond never sees plaintext.
- **Outer control plane** (PROBE, CONTROL): PSK-HMAC authenticated + per-peer
  monotonic anti-replay — an attacker cannot forge or replay them.
- **Outer data plane** (DATA, PARITY): **unauthenticated by design**. A network
  attacker can forge/replay these; forgeries are dropped by the inner AEAD (real
  payload) or discarded by the FEC decoder as inconsistent. The accepted residual
  risk is **DoS-grade only** (an attacker can waste decode/resequence work), never
  a confidentiality or integrity break. This trade buys ~0 per-packet overhead on
  the hot path.
- **Traffic analysis / DPI**: the outer wire has no fingerprint (random nonce,
  obfuscated body, no magic bytes); AmneziaWG junk params add defense-in-depth.
  Protocol *mimicry* (looking like HTTPS) is an explicit non-goal.

  authenticated DATA/PARITY from a not-yet-bound source carries no binding
  authority and is dropped until a PROBE binds it. A forged/garbage frame
  verifies under no psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.

(views atomic.Pointer) at every fan-out site, so the demux reads it lock-free.
handleInbound keeps its byte-identical per-view contract (decode under the
view's codec, dispatch to ps.peer); the single-peer edge/hub takes the
demuxInbound fast path (<=1 view) and is unchanged. No DATA/PARITY wire change.
